column1: Avg Fire Risk Score
column3: A numerical value or rating that represents the likelihood of a property experiencing a fire loss. Insurers use this score to assess risk, determine premiums, and make underwriting decisions.

column1: Avg PPC
column2: PPC ratings range from 1 (best protection) to 10 (worst protection).
column3: Public Protection Classification. The Public Protection Classification is a rating system developed by the Insurance Services Office (ISO) in the United States. It measures the effectiveness of local fire departments and their ability to protect the community.

CAT (Catastrophe) in Insurance: 
Coverage A (Cov A) in insurance—most commonly referenced in homeowners insurance policies—refers to "Dwelling Coverage." This is the portion of your insurance policy that covers the physical structure of your home against covered perils (such as fire, windstorm, hail, lightning, etc.).

CAT (Catastrophe) in Insurance:
Coverage C (Cov C) in insurance—most commonly found in homeowners insurance policies—refers to Personal Property Coverage. This part of your policy protects your personal belongings against covered perils, such as fire, theft, vandalism, and certain types of water damage.

column1: Earned Exposure
column3:
Earned exposure is the amount of risk that an insurance company has covered during a given period, typically measured in units such as car-years, policy-years, or house-years.
"Exposure" is a unit representing one insured item (car, house, person, etc.) for a set period (usually one year).
"Earned" means the portion of the exposure that has already occurred, as opposed to "unearned exposure," which refers to future periods still covered by the policy.
Purpose: Used to match premiums earned with the risk actually covered, enabling accurate loss ratio and profitability calculations.
Example:
If an insurer writes a 12-month auto policy for one car starting January 1, by June 30, they have earned 0.5 car-years of exposure (half the policy term).
If a policy is canceled after 3 months, the insurer has earned 0.25 policy-years of exposure.

column1: Earned Premium
column3:
Earned Premium:
The amount of premium that corresponds to the elapsed portion of an insurance policy. It reflects the revenue the insurer has "earned" for providing insurance coverage up to a certain date. Earned premium = Portion of premium for which coverage has already been provided.
Unearned premium = Portion of premium for which coverage is still to be provided.
Insurers report earned premium as revenue on their income statements.
